title: How the National Gallery of Art Used Video to Take an Exhibition Beyond Its Walls
content:
LaToya Hobbs filmed working in her Baltimore Studio

A multi-platform video strategy built around artist Elizabeth Catlett shows how documentary storytelling, time-lapse filmmaking, and digital installation can turn an exhibition into a living, cross-cultural conversation.

When the National Gallery of Art’s production studio started planning content around its Elizabeth Catlett exhibition — a travelling retrospective that opened in Washington, D.C. in March 2025 — the team wasn’t simply asking how to promote a show. They were asking something more fundamental: how do you honour a legacy this powerful in a way that feels equally powerful?

The answer they arrived at, presented at the

creator: Manual Charr

title: Designing for the Classroom: How Tate Schools Is Rethinking Digital Learning with Art
content:
What happens when a national museum stops asking “how do we make our collection available online?” and starts asking “what do teachers actually need?” The answer, as Tate’s Effie Coe explained at the Museum Digital Summit 2026, looks quite different from most museum digital education platforms.

When Tate set out to build a new digital learning platform for schools, the team began with a deliberately uncomfortable question: why would teachers want to engage with art through digital spaces at all? It was an honest starting point — and it set the tone for everything that followed.

The result is Tate Schools, a platform developed between 2023 and 2025 through a major digital infrastructure project and close collaboration between Tate’s Learning and Digital teams. Effie Coe, Curator of Digital Content in the schools and teachers team, presented the project at the Museum Digital Summit 2026, walking deleg
snippet: What happens when a national museum stops asking “how do we make our collection available online?” and starts asking “what do teachers actually need?” The answer, as Tate’s Effie Coe explained at the
creator: Manual Charr

title: How Portsmouth Museum Took Art to the Parks, Cemeteries and Communities That Needed It Most
content:
What does meaningful community engagement look like when you strip away the grand budgets and the elaborate programming? For Portsmouth Museum & Art Gallery, it looked like a tuk-tuk, a backpack full of pencils, and a willingness to meet people exactly where they were.

Between August 2024 and September 2025, the museum ran Unlocking the Quietness of Feeling — a community programme built around a permanent collection of landscape paintings by Benjamin Horton, whose daughter donated around 300 works to the museum in 1973. Over the course of the project, the team engaged 2,500 residents across the city through 75 nature-based and art activities. Not in the gallery. Out in parks, gardens, cemeteries and community centres. With toddlers, young carers, people with dementia, refugees, adults with SEND needs, and families eligible for free school meals.

At a recent MuseumNext session, Grace Scott and Mathilde Chanvin from Portsmouth Museum Service presented the project as a case stud
snippet: What does meaningful community engagement look like when you strip away the grand budgets and the elaborate programming? For Portsmouth Museum & Art Gallery, it looked like a tuk-tuk, a backpack f
creator: Manual Charr

title: How the Canadian Museum of History Is Embedding Sustainability Into Its DNA
content:
From geothermal river water to annual environmental reporting, Canada’s national museum of human history is treating sustainability as a mindset, not a project.

When the Canadian Museum of History was designed in the 1980s, its architects made a decision that was, in the words of its current Head of Mechanical and Electrical Technology, “ahead of its time by 20 years.” Rather than installing conventional cooling towers on the roof, they chose to use water drawn from the adjacent Ottawa River to cool the building’s mechanical systems. Decades later, that original commitment to environmental thinking is being matched by a new, organisation-wide push to embed sustainability into every corner of the museum’s operations.

A Building Designed Around Its Environment

The Museum of History sits on the shores of the Ottawa River in Gatineau, Quebec, and its relationship with that
snippet: From geothermal river water to annual environmental reporting, Canada’s national museum of human history is treating sustainability as a mindset, not a project. When the Canadian Museum of History was
creator: Jim Richardson

title: When the Museum Becomes a Space for Emotions: Inside the Biosphère’s Emolab
content:
Montréal’s Biosphère has created an exhibition dedicated entirely to climate emotions — and the story of how it came to exist is almost as compelling as the exhibition itself.

The moment that led to the Biosphère’s most ambitious recent project was not a strategic planning session or a market research report. It was a meeting that went unexpectedly off-script. Staff were gathered to brainstorm the future of a gallery space that needed refreshing. And then, for reasons that Eve-Lyne Cayouette Ashby, the museum’s head of exhibitions, programmes and education, still can’t fully explain, something shifted.

“We started sharing our feelings, our emotions about environmental issues and the crisis. We started opening up amongst ourselves.”

It was, she reflects, the first time this group of museum professionals — people who spent their working liv

creator: Manual Charr

title: What Happens When Museums Turn Mindfulness Inward
content:
The Getty has spent years helping visitors slow down and connect with art. Now it is applying those same practices to the people who work there — with results that reach from daily stress management to the science of conservation itself.

There is a particular irony in the museum sector’s relationship with mindfulness. Institutions have invested significantly in helping visitors arrive more fully — to breathe, to look slowly, to be present with a work of art. And yet the professionals who make those experiences possible are often racing between meetings, managing complex logistics, and carrying the weight of a demanding working environment. The Getty has started doing something about that.

Turning the Practice Inward

Since 2015, Getty Museum educator Lilit Sadoyan has been at the forefront of integrating mindfulness into encounters with art for public audiences. Over the years, she has worked with underserved youth, medical students,
snippet: The Getty has spent years helping visitors slow down and connect with art. Now it is applying those same practices to the people who work there — with results that reach from daily stress management t
creator: Manual Charr

title: How the Vancouver Art Gallery Is Turning Art Into a Prescription for Wellbeing
content:
By partnering with Canada’s national nature prescription programme, the Vancouver Art Gallery has created something genuinely new: a social prescribing initiative that brings art, nature, and community care together under one roof.

There’s a thought that many people have standing in front of a painting that moves them — that this, somehow, is good for you. The Vancouver Art Gallery has decided to take that thought seriously, and to build institutional structures around it. The result is the Art of Wellbeing Lab: an evolving initiative that is repositioning the gallery as an active participant in public health, not merely a passive backdrop to it.

A Response to Place

The Art of Wellbeing Lab didn’t begin with a programme design. It began with a question: what does it mean to genuinely respond to the place you’re in?
snippet: By partnering with Canada’s national nature prescription programme, the Vancouver Art Gallery has created something genuinely new: a social prescribing initiative that brings art, nature, and communit
creator: Manual Charr

title: How a Tokyo Science Museum Used Behavioural Science to Change Its Own Staff’s Habits
content:
Miraikan ran a four-month campaign to encourage employees to swap disposables for reusables. Here’s what they learned about making sustainability behaviour change actually work.

When sustainability teams think about who they need to reach, visitors are usually the obvious answer. But at Miraikan, The National Museum of Emerging Science and Innovation in Tokyo, the sustainability promotion team came to a different conclusion: before you can credibly ask the world to change, you have to start closer to home.

The result was an internal campaign, running from March to June 2025, that asked not visitors but Miraikan’s own employees to rethink their relationship with disposable cups, bottles, chopsticks, and cutlery. It’s a deceptively simple idea, and the way the team approached it — rigorously, realistically, and with a healthy dose of fun — offers a model worth looking at carefully.

Starting with the Science

creator: Manual Charr

title: When the Museum Comes to Prison: Oxford’s ‘In the Frame’ Programme
content:
How the University of Oxford’s Gardens, Libraries and Museums is using handling objects and creative activities to support wellbeing inside HMP Bullingdon and HMP Grendon.

What happens when a museum removes its walls entirely — and brings its objects into one of the most challenging environments imaginable? For the community engagement team at the University of Oxford Gardens, Libraries and Museums (GLAM), the answer has been a programme called In the Frame, and the results have been quietly remarkable.

From Pilot to Programme

GLAM’s work in the criminal justice sector didn’t begin with a polished blueprint. Since 2022, the team had been running occasional sessions in Bullingdon Prison, bringing handling objects in to stimulate conversation. A social prescribing pilot followed, run in partnership with NHS England Health and Justice — but as community engagement officer Sarah Levete acknowledges,

creator: Manual Charr

title: How the Art Institute of Chicago Is Building Mindfulness Into the Museum Experience
content:
What happens when you design for “how do I be with the art?” rather than “what is the art?” — and why a live gallery programme and a digital audio tour are better together than either could be alone.

There’s a question that museum professionals have been circling for years: how do you make a visit feel personally meaningful, rather than simply educational? For Marielle Epstein, Assistant Director of Interpretation at the Art Institute of Chicago, the answer came from an unexpected direction — mindfulness.

Want more insights like this? Explore our upcoming virtual conferences here

 A Different Kind of Question

The Art Institute welcomes nearly 1.5 million visitors each year. Its collection spans centuries and cultures, and its programmes are wide-ranging. But Epstein and her colleague Gina Giambalvo, the museum’s Digital Studio Director, f
snippet: What happens when you design for “how do I be with the art?” rather than “what is the art?” — and why a live gallery programme and a digital audio tour are better together than either could be alone.
creator: Manual Charr

title: Art, Empathy, and Healthcare Worker Wellbeing at the Whitney Museum
content:
The Whitney Museum of American Art has built two distinctive partnerships with New York hospital systems — and the results are transforming how medical professionals relate to patients, colleagues, and themselves.

There’s a question that rarely gets asked in discussions about healthcare: who looks after the people doing the looking after? Since the pandemic, awareness of mental health provision for patients has grown significantly. But the wellbeing of the healthcare workers themselves — the nurses, surgeons, researchers, administrators, and students holding the system together — has often been an afterthought. The Whitney Museum of American Art in New York is trying to change that.

Two Partnerships, One Mission

Through two distinct programmes, the Whitney has built meaningful collaborations with major hospital organisations in New York City. The first is with NYC Health + Hospitals, the largest hospital network in New York, which pro
snippet: The Whitney Museum of American Art has built two distinctive partnerships with New York hospital systems — and the results are transforming how medical professionals relate to patients, colleagues, an
creator: Manual Charr

title: When the Museum Becomes a Group: Stapferhaus’s Experiment in Belonging
content:
How a Swiss museum partnered with a healthcare clinic to create a space where strangers could explore what it means to connect.

There’s a question that sits at the heart of contemporary public life: in a world that feels increasingly fragmented, how do we move from “me” to “we”? For the Stapferhaus in Lenzburg, Switzerland, that question became not just a curatorial theme but the basis for an unusually intimate experiment — one that asked thirty strangers to sit in a circle, without a script or a plan, and see what happened.

An Unlikely Collaboration

The Stapferhaus describes itself as a place for encounter and dialogue. Its current exhibition, Health: An Exhibition with Side Effects, invites visitors to explore the many dimensions of wellbeing — what health means, who defines it, and how societies might build systems th
snippet: How a Swiss museum partnered with a healthcare clinic to create a space where strangers could explore what it means to connect. There’s a question that sits at the heart of contemporary public life: i
creator: Manual Charr

title: National Gallery Singapore, Youth People and Mental Health
content:
What if an art museum’s role extended beyond curating masterpieces and educating school groups? What if its platforms — its walls, its festivals, its expertise — could be turned over to young people who are rarely seen, let alone heard? That’s the question at the heart of a collaboration between National Gallery Singapore and social service agency Fei Yue Community Services, presented at a recent MuseumNext conference by Mian Tze Kng and Zoe Tee.

From School Programmes to the Margins

National Gallery Singapore has, since 2021, been developing Strength Through Art (stART), a programme designed to improve the visual and emotional literacy of young people. In its original form, stART was delivered to youth in mainstream schools, following a three-phase framework: Name, Frame, and Reframe.
